exemplar.title: An investigation into how the friction coefficient and forces of different materials vary across  floor surfaces and the implications for their practical use

detailedExemplarData.title: An investigation into how the friction coefficient and forces of different materials vary across  floor surfaces and the implications for their practical use

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data and processed tables are well labelled, with units, sample calculations, and error bars.
Averages and propagated uncertainties are calculated correctly and coefficients of friction are derived accurately.
Equations are presented precisely, with variables defined, supporting transparent data processing.
weaknesses: Uncertainty in the normal force is not included in propagation.
The averaging method (maxâmin/2) is simplistic and omits statistical measures.
Inconsistent significant figures and unit notation reduce overall precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Communication and processing are clear and precise (band 5), but the treatment of uncertainties is only partially appropriate (band 4).  The one lower strand caps the overall Data Analysis score at 4.

strengths: Introduction situates the question in a clear, specific context (ergonomics, material science, interior design).
Comprehensive equipment list and step-by-step numbered procedure allow reproducibility.
Variables (independent, dependent, controlled) are clearly identified, justified, and controlled.
weaknesses: Minor phrasing and formatting issues in mass description (e.g., mismatched parentheses).
Uncontrolled variable list omits rope elasticity, which could affect force transmission.
Step 10 lacks a precise method (e.g., protractor guide) to ensure a consistent pull angle.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the topâband descriptors (5â6) with clear context, reproducible methodology and explained methodological considerations. Minor wording/formatting issues and one missing uncontrolled variable warrant a score at the lower end of the top band.

strengths: Conclusion correctly restates main numerical trends and links them to material properties.
Practical implications (e.g., grip hierarchies) are identified and consistent with analysis.
weaknesses: Comparison to accepted literature values or expected Î¼ ranges is minimal.
Quantitative justification against safety thresholds or incorporation of uncertainties is missing.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is consistent with the data (band 4) but only shows âsome relevant comparisonâ to scientific context (band 3).  The lower strand assessment limits the holistic score to 3.

strengths: Specific methodological limitations are identified, with clear explanations of their impact on results.
Realistic and relevant improvements (e.g., motorized pull, higher sampling rate) are proposed with rationale.
weaknesses: Errors are not ranked by relative impact, which would deepen the evaluation.
Feasibility and resource requirements for suggested improvements in a typical school lab are not discussed.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both strands meet the topâband criteria (5â6) in identifying specific weaknesses and proposing relevant improvements.  Minor omissions (error ranking, feasibility discussion) recommend a score of 5 rather than a perfect 6.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student introduces the investigation by explicitly linking the chosen materials and floor types to concrete fields such as ergonomics and interior design, and then narrows the focus to the particular interaction between furniture pads and two named surfaces.  This establishes a clear, specific and relevant context rather than merely stating a topic.  Minor phrasing issues do not detract from the fact that readers immediately understand why these particular surfaces and materials matter, satisfying the descriptor for the highest band.

Instrument uncertainty is stated, range/2 spread is used, and propagation to Î¼ is demonstrated.  Error bars appear on graphs.  However, uncertainty in the normal force is ignored, and the averaging method for ÎF uses only maxâmin/2 rather than statistical measures.  These omissions indicate partial but not fully âappropriateâ treatment, placing the work in band 3-4.

Independent, dependent, controlled and uncontrolled variables are organised in tables with explanations of significance and means of control; the student also justifies choices (e.g., equalising block mass, wiping surfaces).  The rationale behind each control is explicit, demonstrating an explanation rather than a mere list.  Hence the work aligns with the âexplainedâ requirement of the top band.

Specific weaknesses (e.g., sensor sampling rate, manual pulling, surface wear) are identified, and their directional impact on friction values is explained, demonstrating understanding of how each factor could inflate or deflate results.  This explanatory level satisfies the top-band descriptor.

A full equipment list, step-by-step numbered procedure, trial repetition schedule and data-logging settings (3-s collection, LabQuest, sensor Â±0.1 N) are provided.  Someone with similar school-lab resources could replicate the work without needing to infer critical details.  Ambiguities are limited to minor formatting issues (e.g., â119) gramsâ) and do not prevent reproduction, so the standard for band 5-6 is met.

Averages, propagated uncertainties and coefficients are calculated correctly; units are consistent and figures plotted logically.  No major inaccuracies are evident, and the processing steps are appropriate for the research question.  Consequently the strand meets the requirements for accurate processing in the highest band.

For every cited weakness, a realistic and relevant improvement is provided (e.g., motorised pulling system, increased sampling rate, controlled environment), with a clear explanation of how the change would mitigate the error.  The depth and practicality of these proposals fulfil the requirements for the highest band.

Raw data are displayed in well-labelled tables; processed data and graphs are likewise clearly labelled with units and uncertainties.  Sample calculations are shown.  Although some typographical issues exist, both clarity (readers can follow what each number represents) and precision (units, significant figures, error bars) are generally maintained, fulfilling the top-band descriptor.

The student connects findings to practical uses and briefly states that cork and rubber exhibit higher grip as expected, but there is minimal comparison to textbook or literature Î¼ values despite citing sources.  The discussion therefore shows âsome relevant comparisonâ but is not thoroughly justified against accepted scientific context, meriting the lower half of band 3-4.

The conclusion restates main numerical trends (e.g., cork highest on parquet, general ranking) and links them to material properties, showing consistency with processed data.  Nevertheless, justification is largely qualitative; no quantitative comparison to uncertainties or statistical significance is made.  This aligns with a âdescribed but not fully justifiedâ conclusion, i.e., upper middle band.

annotations.comment: The introduction effectively situates the research in ergonomics, material science, and interior design, demonstrating a clear and specific context that directly supports the research questionâs relevance.
Uncontrolled variables list omits rope elasticity, which may affect force transmission. Include rope stiffness or material properties under uncontrolled variables.
The mass description âEach equaled 119) grams)â has mismatched parentheses and unclear wording. Revise for clarity (e.g., âEach block weighed 0.119âkgâ).
Step 10 instructs maintaining a consistent angle but lacks a method to measure it. Use a protractor or fixed guide to ensure the pull is truly parallel and reproducible.
The controlled variables table comprehensively lists environmental, surface, weight, and duration controls, demonstrating clear consideration for reproducibility.
Preliminary trials description clearly identifies mass inconsistency and corrective action, reflecting thoughtful refinement of methodology before full data collection.
Tile data table lacks units on force values (e.g., N). Always include units in table headings to maintain clarity and avoid misinterpretation.
The qualitative observations enrich the numeric data, but integrating specific sensor peaks and their corresponding Î¼ values would deepen the analysis.
The average friction force calculation is presented clearly with formula and values, facilitating understanding of data processing steps.
The section header â1.2 Research Ouestionâ contains a typo. Correct the spelling to maintain professional clarity and avoid distracting readers from the research focus.

The background describes friction qualitatively but does not link microscopic roughness explicitly to the coefficient of friction formula. Expand this section by explaining how asperity interactions determine Î¼_k.
Equation (1) is clearly presented, with each variable defined in accompanying list items. This precision aids reproducibility and data processing clarity.
The discussion of terminal velocity and drag in a sliding friction investigation is misplaced and may confuse readers. Remove or relocate this content to maintain focus on surface interactions.
The unit annotation for normal force includes âN/kg, m s^{-2}â which is confusing. Standardize to âN (kgÂ·mÂ·sâ»Â²)â or simply âNâ to avoid unit notation errors.
The hypothesis is descriptive but could be strengthened with a quantitative prediction of expected Î¼ values or force differences based on literature to guide the analysis.

The manipulated/measured variables table lacks clear column alignment and apparatus entries appear disconnected. Relabel and arrange columns so each variable, its explanation, and apparatus are unambiguously linked.
Raw data tables for parquet are well-labelled and structured, but inconsistent decimal places detract from precision. Standardize to two decimal places to match sensor resolution.
Uncertainty propagation uses (maxâmin)/2 but neglects the instrumentâs Â±0.1 N and any normal force uncertainty. Include all relevant sources for a more complete error analysis.
Processed data table shows means and uncertainties accurately, but inconsistent significant figures (e.g., Â±0.065 vs Â±0.05) reduce precision. Standardize notation.
Tile friction averages table applies a generic Â±0.1 N rather than calculated uncertainties. Use statistical methods (e.g., standard deviation) to derive uncertainty values.
Coefficient of friction calculation for felt is correct and clearly shown, including the ratio formula, supporting transparent data processing.
Uncertainty in Î¼ is calculated via ÎF/F_N but omits propagation formula for division. Use full propagation: ÎÎ¼ = Î¼ Â· (ÎF/F + ÎF_N/F_N) for rigor.
Parquet Î¼ table is well-structured and presents coefficients with uncertainties in an accessible format, facilitating comparison across materials.
Tile Î¼ table omits any comparison to literature values or expected Î¼ ranges. Include a discussion contrasting these results with accepted context to justify findings.
The caption âPlotting the data on the graph using an Excel sheetâ is vague. Specify axes, units, and include error bars to improve figure clarity and analytical value.
Graphs lack trendline equations and RÂ² values. Adding these would enable quantitative interpretation of the linear relationship between friction force and coefficient.
The concluding paragraph trails off mid-sentence. Ensure full, coherent statements summarizing key trends and their implications for readability.
The conclusion recommends material uses but does not link the numerical Î¼ differences to practical safety thresholds (e.g., minimum Î¼ for slip resistance). Quantify these criteria.
Weaknesses tables list random errors but do not discuss which has the greatest impact. Rank or explain each errorâs relative influence to deepen evaluation.
Systematic error improvements are practical but lack discussion of implementation feasibility in a school lab context. Explain resource requirements or procedural steps.
The strengths table cites âReference to existing literature,â but earlier discussion did not compare or validate measured Î¼ against literature values. Address this inconsistency.

rubricData.name: Physics IA
rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
